chapters.text: September 3, 1977. Raceway Park, Englishtown, New Jersey. The largest crowd the Grateful Dead would ever play to — 150,000 people on a hot Labor Day weekend, the band at the absolute peak of their Wall of Sound era.
Set one opens with Promised Land — Garcia, Weir, Lesh, the rhythm devils, and Keith and Donna Godchaux all in the prime of their working life together.
Mississippi Half-Step. The summer of '77 made this song a peak of the Dead's catalog. Listen for the way the band lifts on the chorus.
Looks Like Rain. Bob Weir's prayer to the elements — and on this Labor Day weekend, with 150,000 people in a New Jersey field, the song reads as something more than a love song.
Set one closer — The Music Never Stopped. By 1977 the band had this song dialed: Bob's funky rhythm, Garcia's ascending leads, the whole field dancing.
Set break. The largest crowd in Dead history settles in. Hot September afternoon turning toward evening.
Set two: Bertha into Good Lovin'. The Englishtown crowd is fully tuned in — the Dead are in their second decade now, and tonight the band knows what to give them.
Estimated Prophet > Eyes of the World. The summer of 1977 was the high-water mark of these songs — the version of Eyes that grows out of Estimated tonight is one of the canonical recordings.
He's Gone. Written for Lenny Hart, Mickey's father — and now five years on, the song belongs to whoever needs it. The harmonies on the bridge tonight are as tight as the band ever got.
Not Fade Away. Buddy Holly into the Grateful Dead's hands becomes a 30-minute chant. 150,000 voices on the chorus.
Encore: Terrapin Station. The Dead's most ambitious composition, in its first summer of regular performance. Garcia singing about the lady with the fan.

narration.text: Estimated Prophet — Weir's Old Testament cowboy preacher, three years after he wrote it, peaked in the summer of '77.
Eyes of the World. The peak version of the peak summer of one of the great rock bands.
